title: JTBD Analysis — Hotels (except Casino Hotels) and Motels

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, and Offering Level

| NAICS | CFJ | Output Type | Offering Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 721110 | Provide paid temporary accommodation that meets guests' comfort, safety, and accessibility needs while maintaining full regulatory compliance with fire safety and building codes | Full-service hotel (upscale/luxury) | L7 (Ecosystem) |
| | | Select-service hotel (branded mid-scale) | L7 |
| | | Limited-service / budget hotel / motel | L7 |
| | | Extended-stay hotel | L7 |

**Routing:** L7 Ecosystem → service NAICS. Phases 1.4 (Product BOM) and 1.5 (Edges) are skipped — no physical output; service composition applies. Emergency exit monitoring activates universally across all four output types.

### 1.2 Segments

Circumscribed by **operational context** (different alternatives and trade-offs), not demographics.

| # | Segment | Defining Circumstance | Why distinct |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Multi-property hotel chain (portfolio) | Manages 10–500+ properties with centralized security oversight | Needs cross-property monitoring aggregation; individual property economics differ |
| S2 | Full-service / upscale single property | High brand-standard scrutiny; architect-designed interiors; integrated BMS common | Aesthetic sensitivity (C5 surface mount friction); BMS protocol integration required (C6) |
| S3 | Select-service / mid-scale property | Retrofit budget approved; cost-ROI driven; limited in-house facilities staff | Commissioning ease and IoT dashboard value proposition are primary evaluation criteria |
| S4 | Budget / limited-service motel | Minimal infrastructure; no BMS; price-sensitive; owner-operated | IoT premium (C7) is barrier; mechanical-only may dominate; simple deployment required |
| S5 | Extended-stay property | Semi-residential guest tenure (7–30+ days); reduced after-hours staffing | Battery life more critical (long cycles between maintenance); semi-public exit use patterns |

**Anti-pattern avoided:** "Large hotel vs. small hotel" is demographics. S1–S5 are circumstance-defined.

### 1.3 Process Value Network

Full VN documented in `VN_hotels_motels.md`. Key nodes:

| L6 Node | Job Family | Türwächter Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| L6H-SEC Building Security & Life Safety | Protect occupants, assets, and escape routes at all times | **DIRECT ANCHOR** — product enters at L5 Emergency Exit Security Devices |
| L6H-FM Facility Management & Maintenance | Maintain physical plant and utilities in operational condition | Adjacent — BMS integration and maintenance scheduling |
| L6b Guest Arrival & Check-in | Process guest arrival, assign room, issue access credentials | Indirect — access control context at same security layer |

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Anchor unit** | Emergency Exit Security Devices |
| **Anchor level** | L5 (standalone product/system) |
| **Parent VN unit** | Building Security & Life Safety (L6H-SEC) |
| **Parent level** | L6 (horizontal process) |
| **Anchor type** | PROCESS_VN — product is used by the hotel operator; it does not end up inside the accommodation output |
| **Alternative slot** | Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring (~25% market share) |
| **Key constraints active** | C5 (surface mount — luxury), C6 (EasyWave protocol — BMS), C7 (price premium — budget), C8 (GDPR — EU hotels) |

### 2.2 Lens

**Function verb** from L6H-SEC job_family: *protect*

> "What must hotels be able to **protect** emergency exit doors against unauthorized use [in the context of Building Security & Life Safety] while maintaining full fire-code-compliant escape capability?"

Segments filtered to: S1, S2, S3, S5 (primary); S4 (budget) is adjacent — product relevance lower but not absent.

### 2.3 Core Job and Product Job

| Level | Type | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| L6H-SEC parent | Core Functional Job (CFJ) | Provide paid temporary accommodation that meets guests' comfort, safety, and accessibility needs while maintaining full regulatory compliance with fire safety and building codes |
| L5 anchor | **Product Job** | Help Building Security & Life Safety to detect and deter unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded fire-code-compliant escape capability across all floors and wings |

**Abstraction test:** 3+ alternatives from different categories exist (mechanical alarm, wired panic bar, security guard patrols, access-control integration, do nothing) → confirmed Core Job at this level.

### 2.4 Alternatives

| # | Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Basic mechanical door alarm (no monitoring) — 45% share | 46171610 | No remote visibility; staff must physically patrol to detect unreported propping; no audit log; battery-free |
| A2 | Wired panic bar / crash bar alarm — 20% share | 46171601 | Requires mains wiring conduit to each door; high retrofit cost in existing buildings; no wireless aggregation |
| A3 | **Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring** (Türwächter) — 25% share | 46171601 | Proprietary wireless protocol (C6); surface-mount profile visible (C5); battery dependency (C4); ~730 EUR per door (C7) |
| A4 | Security guard patrols | 92121500 | Labor-intensive; coverage gaps at shift changes; no event log; scales linearly with door count; high recurring cost |
| A5 | No dedicated exit monitoring (do nothing) — 10% share | — | Zero installation burden; zero P1 burden; full compliance and brand audit risk; undetected misuse propagates |

**"Do nothing" is A5** — zero trade-offs, zero P1 burden; explains 10% market position despite clear regulatory exposure.

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| # | Scenario | Output type activated | Alternatives in play |
|---|---|---|---|
| SC1 | Guest propping stairwell exit door for balcony/roof access or smoking | All four | A1, A3, A4 |
| SC2 | Staff propping service exit for delivery shortcut | All four | A1, A3, A4 |
| SC3 | Night audit: minimal staffing, all floors unsupervised | FS, SS, ES | A3, A4 |
| SC4 | Multi-property chain: centralized security team monitors remote portfolio | FS, SS | A3 (IoT only) |
| SC5 | Annual fire marshal / brand standards audit | All four | A1, A2, A3 (documentation requirement) |
| SC6 | Renovation / retrofit: replace outdated wired door alarms without structural work | FS, SS | A3 vs. A2 |

**Sparsity check:** SC4 only activates FS/SS (not BU/ES at multi-property chain level) → Core Job is granular enough, no split required.

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Person in 721110 | Pyramid levels | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Job Executor | Security officer / night duty manager | P1 (all), P2, P3, P4, P5 | Operates dashboard, responds to alerts |
| Job Overseer | Director of Security / Hotel GM | P1 (own), P2, P4 | Accountable for compliance KPIs; franchise audits |
| Job Beneficiary | Hotel guests (and staff) | P2, P5 | Safe evacuation; unimpeded escape |
| Lifecycle Support — Specifier | Facilities consultant / engineer | P1 Acquisition | Defines specification for renovation project |
| Lifecycle Support — Installer | Maintenance technician / security integrator | P1 Preparation | Commissions hardware; pairs devices |
| Lifecycle Support — Validator | Fire marshal / building inspector | P1 Confirm | Signs off on escape route compliance |
| Lifecycle Support — Maintainer | Hotel maintenance team | P1 Maintenance | Battery replacement; dashboard health checks |
| Purchase Executor | Facilities/Engineering director | P1 Acquisition | Approves CAPEX; ROI evaluation |
| Purchase Influencer | Insurance underwriter; brand standards auditor | P2, P4 | Sets compliance requirements; influences specification |

### 3.1 Burleson Pyramid — P1 Product Jobs per Alternative

| Stage | A1 Basic mech. alarm | A2 Wired panic bar | A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter) | A4 Guard patrol | A5 Do nothing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquire** | Specify per-door unit; quote; order | Engage integrator; wiring design; structural survey; permitting | Specify device+gateway; assess RF coverage; quote; certify compatibility with BMS | Issue RFP; evaluate guard firm; SLA negotiation | — |
| **Prepare** | Surface-mount per door; key provisioning | Install conduit; pull wire; mount bar; panel programming | Mount device; pair to gateway; configure zones; test alarm + radio | Define patrol schedules; brief staff; configure incident log | — |
| **Use** | Manual patrol to verify no propping; respond to audible alarm if heard | Alarm panel event response; physical site investigation | Dashboard monitoring; alert triage; investigation dispatch | Guard responds in person per schedule or on-call | Accept compliance gap |
| **Maintain** | Periodic tamper check; no battery required | Annual panel service; wiring inspection | Battery replacement (~every 2 yrs); firmware update; signal level check | Staff turnover; re-training; scheduling gaps | — |
| **Dispose** | Remove hardware; patch door | Remove hardware; remove conduit; patch walls | Remove device; decommission from dashboard | Contract termination | — |

**Key insight:** A3 (Türwächter) creates the highest Acquisition and Preparation P1 burden (RF survey, BMS compatibility check, gateway pairing) but the lowest Use-stage burden — security officer monitors remotely without patrol. A5 (Do nothing) has **zero P1 burden** — explains its persistence despite compliance risk.

### 3.2 Job Map — 8 Universal Steps

| # | Step | Statement (hotel context) | Relevant to product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | Determine which emergency exit doors require monitoring, alert thresholds, notification rules, and coverage zones | YES — configuration scope |
| 2 | LOCATE | Identify all emergency exit points across floors; assess wireless signal paths; map gateway placement | YES — RF coverage planning |
| 3 | PREPARE | Install monitoring devices on door handles; pair to gateway; configure zones; test alarm tone | YES — commissioning |
| 4 | CONFIRM | Verify every device transmits correctly, alarm is unobstructed, and escape motion is unrestricted | YES — acceptance testing |
| 5 | EXECUTE | Maintain all exits in secured state 24/7; trigger alert on unauthorized activation | YES — core function |
| 6 | MONITOR | Track real-time exit door status from centralized dashboard; receive alerts; correlate with CCTV or BMS | YES — primary value |
| 7 | MODIFY | Respond to and reset triggered devices; adjust alert rules; replace failed units | YES — operational |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Generate compliance audit reports; log fire marshal inspections; replace low-battery units proactively | YES — compliance output |

### 3.3 Error Statements — P1 + P2 (Seven Error Types)

| # | Full Statement | Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | OS | Impact | p_related | Confidence | Underserved |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | Minimize the likelihood of high-risk emergency exits remaining unmonitored due to incomplete upfront coverage mapping, leaving ≥1 undetected propping event per month | DEFINE | DETECTION | 7 | 5 | 9 | safety | false | 0.70 | — |
| E2 | Increase the ability to determine wireless receiver placement achieving >95% floor-level signal coverage before hardware purchase or installation | LOCATE | ACCURACY | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | **true** | 0.70 | — |
| E3 | Minimize the time required to install and commission emergency exit monitoring devices across a 100-room hotel to <4 hours without structural door modification | PREPARE | EFFORT | 8 | 5 | 11 | time | **true** | 0.75 | — |
| E4 | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit alarm functionality being impaired during device mounting, pairing, or initial configuration | PREPARE | ROBUSTNESS | 9 | 7 | 11 | safety | false | 0.80 | — |
| E5 | Increase the ability to verify that every installed exit monitoring device is transmitting correctly and that the audible alarm operates without obstruction — within a single end-to-end commissioning test | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | 9 | 5 | 13 | reliability | false | 0.80 | ✓ |
| E6 | Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit remaining in an unauthorized open state for >5 minutes without triggering a staff alert | EXECUTE | DETECTION | 9 | 4 | 14 | safety | false | 0.85 | ✓ |
| E7 | Minimize the likelihood of legitimate authorized staff uses of emergency exits (housekeeping, maintenance) generating false alerts that erode security staff confidence in the monitoring system | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | 8 | 4 | 12 | reliability | false | 0.80 | ✓ |
| E8 | Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit monitoring device adding any additional step or force requirement that delays occupant evacuation during a fire emergency | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | 10 | 8 | 12 | safety | false | 0.90 | — |
| E9 | Increase the ability of a single security officer to monitor real-time exit door status across all floors from one centralized interface without physical patrol — covering ≥50 exit points simultaneously | MONITOR | THROUGHPUT | 9 | 4 | 14 | time | false | 0.85 | ✓ |
| E10 | Minimize the time from emergency exit activation event to security staff notification to <30 seconds regardless of time of day | MONITOR | DETECTION | 9 | 5 | 13 | time | false | 0.85 | ✓ |
| E11 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless connectivity loss causing undetected monitoring gaps exceeding 15 minutes during hotel occupied hours | MONITOR | ROBUSTNESS | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | **true** | 0.75 | — |
| E12 | Minimize the likelihood of the monitoring system misclassifying an emergency evacuation event as an unauthorized door use, triggering an incorrect security response that delays fire evacuation | MONITOR | ACCURACY | 8 | 5 | 11 | safety | false | 0.70 | — |
| E13 | Minimize the time required to reset a triggered exit monitoring device and restore it to active state after investigation, to <2 minutes | MODIFY | EFFORT | 7 | 6 | 8 | time | false | 0.70 | — |
| E14 | Minimize the likelihood of a high-priority unauthorized exit event being missed due to alert saturation from high-frequency low-priority activations | MODIFY | DETECTION | 8 | 5 | 11 | safety | false | 0.75 | — |
| E15 | Increase the ability to produce a complete, timestamped digital audit log of all emergency exit activations and response actions, formatted for fire marshal inspection without manual compilation | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | 8 | 3 | 13 | safety | false | 0.80 | ✓ |
| E16 | Minimize the time required to identify and proactively replace batteries approaching depletion threshold across a deployment of ≥30 exit monitoring devices before monitoring capability is lost | CONCLUDE | DETECTION | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | **true** | 0.75 | — |
| E17 | Minimize the effort required to integrate emergency exit monitoring event data with an existing hotel BMS or security management platform running standard building protocols | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | 7 | 4 | 10 | cost | **true** | 0.70 | — |
| E18 | Minimize the variability in emergency exit compliance status across all floor levels at any given time of day | P2 Core | CONSISTENCY | 8 | 4 | 12 | safety | false | 0.80 | ✓ |
| E19 | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit door management violations being cited during annual fire authority inspection or franchise brand standards audit | P2 Core | ACCURACY | 9 | 6 | 12 | safety | false | 0.80 | — |

**Count:** 19 statements. Product-related=true: E2, E3, E11, E16, E17 = **5/19 = 26%**. Market-inherent=false: **14/19 = 74%**.

**Underserved (imp > 7 AND sat < 5):** E6, E7, E9, E10, E15, E18 — 6 needs.

### 3.4 P3–P5 Bridge Questions

| Level | Role | Retrieval | Bridge | Market Need |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P3 Role | Security officer (executor) | "Becomes the person who runs a systematic, audit-proof safety operation that never fails inspection" | "What must the system do so they CAN demonstrate systematic control?" | Increase the ability to demonstrate evidence-based emergency exit management to insurance underwriters and brand auditors with a single report — without manual data assembly [hypothesis] |
| P4 Image | Hotel GM (overseer) | "Franchise auditors and insurers must see: this property manages safety proactively, not reactively" | "What must the system do so auditors SEE a professional, documented process?" | Minimize the likelihood of a preventable emergency exit failure becoming a documented finding in a franchise brand standards audit [hypothesis] |
| P5 Emotional | Security officer (executor, night shift) | "Wants to feel in control; fears being responsible when an undetected exit breach contributes to a security incident" | "What must the system do to produce the feeling of continuous, reliable coverage even when alone?" | Minimize the sense of uncertainty about whether unmanned emergency exits remain secured during overnight minimum-staffing periods [hypothesis] |

### 3.5 ODI Score Summary

| Rank | Error Statement (short) | OS | Segment priority | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | E6 — Unauthorized exit open >5 min undetected | **14** | S1, S2, S3 | Core value proof point |
| 2 | E9 — Centralized monitoring ≥50 exits, 1 officer | **14** | S1, S2 | Dashboard differentiation |
| 3 | E5 — End-to-end commissioning verification | **13** | S3 | Onboarding simplicity |
| 4 | E10 — Alert latency <30 sec | **13** | S1, S2, S3 | Network reliability |
| 5 | E15 — Digital audit log, fire marshal ready | **13** | S1, S2, S3, S5 | Compliance automation |
| 6 | E18 — Consistency across all floors | **12** | S1, S2 | Portfolio visibility |
| 7 | E7 — False alert fatigue | **12** | S3, S4 | Alert precision tuning |
| 8 | E19 — Fire authority inspection compliance | **12** | All | Regulatory hygiene |

**Top opportunity cluster:** Remote visibility (E6 + E9 + E10 + E18) — the Türwächter's IoT dashboard directly addresses 4 of the 8 top-ranked needs. Compliance documentation (E15) is the highest-satisfaction gap (sat=3) in the set.

---

sections.title: Constraint Impact on Segments
sections.content:
| Constraint | S1 Chain | S2 Upscale | S3 Mid-scale | S4 Budget | S5 Extended |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C5 Surface mount (aesthetics) | — | **Friction** | none | none | none |
| C6 EasyWave protocol lock-in | **Friction** | **Friction** | minor | none | minor |
| C7 IoT price premium | — | none | **ROI case needed** | **Barrier** | minor |
| C8 GDPR (EU properties) | **Required** | required | required | minor | required |
| C4 Battery life | minor | none | minor | none | **Friction** |

No knockout constraints — all are mitigable.

---

sections.title: QA Self-Check
sections.content:
| # | Check | Status |
|---|---|---|
| V1 | All statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…" with ONE measurable KPI | PASS |
| V2 | product_related=true only where need disappears with a different technology (e.g., guard patrol has no battery/RF need) | PASS |
| V3 | No uniform importance/satisfaction pattern; safety/regulatory at 8-10; operational at 7-8 | PASS |
| V4 | Impact category matches KPI (audit log → safety; alert latency → time; battery → reliability) | PASS |
| V5 | ≥2 needs per job step; 5/19 product-specific (26%); 6 underserved identified | PASS |
| V6 | Zero references to EasyWave, 868 MHz, GfS, Türwächter, or piezoelectric in need statements | PASS |
| V7 | Anchor at L5 — standalone system; parent L6H-SEC | PASS |
| V8 | Product job: "Help Building Security & Life Safety to…" includes parent VN unit and context qualifier | PASS |

---

*Sources consulted:*
- [Hotel Security Best Practices — Omnilert](https://www.omnilert.com/blog/best-practices-in-hotel-security)
- [Fire and Life Safety Services Market — GM Insights](https://www.gminsights.com/industry-analysis/fire-and-life-safety-protections-services-market) (commercial sector $71.5B, 2024)
- [Fire Safety Systems Market — Mordor Intelligence](https://www.mordorintelligence.com/industry-reports/fire-safety-systems-market-industry) ($75B in 2025, 6.67% CAGR to 2030)
- [IoT in Fire Protection — Impact Fire Services](https://resources.impactfireservices.com/iot-in-fire-protection)
- [Hotel Fire Protection Guide — Century FP](https://blog.centuryfp.com/hotel-fire-protection-guide-top-5-systems)
- [Emergency Exit Stair Signage for Hotels — Hotel Signs Blog](https://blog.hotelsigns.com/2025/01/01/keeping-guests-and-staff-safe-a-guide-to-emergency-exit-stair-signage-in-hotels/)
- [Panic & Emergency Exit Devices Market — Verified Market Reports](https://www.verifiedmarketreports.com/product/panic-emergency-exit-devices-market-size-and-forecast/)
- VN_hotels_motels.md — internal, confirmed anchor L5 + constraint mapping
- 03_constraints.md — C4, C5, C6, C7, C8 applicability per segment

rows.CFJ: Provide paid temporary accommodation that meets guests' comfort, safety, and accessibility needs while maintaining full regulatory compliance with fire safety and building codes

rows.Defining Circumstance: Manages 10–500+ properties with centralized security oversight
rows.Why distinct: Needs cross-property monitoring aggregation; individual property economics differ

rows.Defining Circumstance: High brand-standard scrutiny; architect-designed interiors; integrated BMS common
rows.Why distinct: Aesthetic sensitivity (C5 surface mount friction); BMS protocol integration required (C6)

rows.Defining Circumstance: Retrofit budget approved; cost-ROI driven; limited in-house facilities staff
rows.Why distinct: Commissioning ease and IoT dashboard value proposition are primary evaluation criteria

rows.Defining Circumstance: Minimal infrastructure; no BMS; price-sensitive; owner-operated
rows.Why distinct: IoT premium (C7) is barrier; mechanical-only may dominate; simple deployment required

rows.Defining Circumstance: Semi-residential guest tenure (7–30+ days); reduced after-hours staffing
rows.Why distinct: Battery life more critical (long cycles between maintenance); semi-public exit use patterns

rows.Job Family: Protect occupants, assets, and escape routes at all times
rows.Türwächter Relevance: **DIRECT ANCHOR** — product enters at L5 Emergency Exit Security Devices

rows.Job Family: Maintain physical plant and utilities in operational condition
rows.Türwächter Relevance: Adjacent — BMS integration and maintenance scheduling

rows.Job Family: Process guest arrival, assign room, issue access credentials
rows.Türwächter Relevance: Indirect — access control context at same security layer

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN — product is used by the hotel operator; it does not end up inside the accommodation output
Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring (~25% market share)
C5 (surface mount — luxury), C6 (EasyWave protocol — BMS), C7 (price premium — budget), C8 (GDPR — EU hotels)

rows.Statement: Provide paid temporary accommodation that meets guests' comfort, safety, and accessibility needs while maintaining full regulatory compliance with fire safety and building codes
Help Building Security & Life Safety to detect and deter unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded fire-code-compliant escape capability across all floors and wings

rows.Alternative: Basic mechanical door alarm (no monitoring) — 45% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: No remote visibility; staff must physically patrol to detect unreported propping; no audit log; battery-free

rows.Alternative: Wired panic bar / crash bar alarm — 20% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Requires mains wiring conduit to each door; high retrofit cost in existing buildings; no wireless aggregation

rows.Alternative: **Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring** (Türwächter) — 25% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Proprietary wireless protocol (C6); surface-mount profile visible (C5); battery dependency (C4); ~730 EUR per door (C7)
Labor-intensive; coverage gaps at shift changes; no event log; scales linearly with door count; high recurring cost

rows.Alternative: No dedicated exit monitoring (do nothing) — 10% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Zero installation burden; zero P1 burden; full compliance and brand audit risk; undetected misuse propagates

rows.Scenario: Guest propping stairwell exit door for balcony/roof access or smoking
Staff propping service exit for delivery shortcut
Night audit: minimal staffing, all floors unsupervised
Multi-property chain: centralized security team monitors remote portfolio
Annual fire marshal / brand standards audit
Renovation / retrofit: replace outdated wired door alarms without structural work

rows.Notes: Accountable for compliance KPIs; franchise audits
Signs off on escape route compliance

rows.A2 Wired panic bar: Engage integrator; wiring design; structural survey; permitting
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Specify device+gateway; assess RF coverage; quote; certify compatibility with BMS
rows.A4 Guard patrol: Issue RFP; evaluate guard firm; SLA negotiation

rows.A2 Wired panic bar: Install conduit; pull wire; mount bar; panel programming
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Mount device; pair to gateway; configure zones; test alarm + radio
rows.A4 Guard patrol: Define patrol schedules; brief staff; configure incident log

rows.A1 Basic mech. alarm: Manual patrol to verify no propping; respond to audible alarm if heard
rows.A2 Wired panic bar: Alarm panel event response; physical site investigation
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Dashboard monitoring; alert triage; investigation dispatch
rows.A4 Guard patrol: Guard responds in person per schedule or on-call

rows.A1 Basic mech. alarm: Periodic tamper check; no battery required
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Battery replacement (~every 2 yrs); firmware update; signal level check

rows.A2 Wired panic bar: Remove hardware; remove conduit; patch walls

rows.Statement (hotel context): Determine which emergency exit doors require monitoring, alert thresholds, notification rules, and coverage zones
Identify all emergency exit points across floors; assess wireless signal paths; map gateway placement
Install monitoring devices on door handles; pair to gateway; configure zones; test alarm tone
Verify every device transmits correctly, alarm is unobstructed, and escape motion is unrestricted
Maintain all exits in secured state 24/7; trigger alert on unauthorized activation
Track real-time exit door status from centralized dashboard; receive alerts; correlate with CCTV or BMS
Respond to and reset triggered devices; adjust alert rules; replace failed units
Generate compliance audit reports; log fire marshal inspections; replace low-battery units proactively

rows.Full Statement: Minimize the likelihood of high-risk emergency exits remaining unmonitored due to incomplete upfront coverage mapping, leaving ≥1 undetected propping event per month
Increase the ability to determine wireless receiver placement achieving >95% floor-level signal coverage before hardware purchase or installation
Minimize the time required to install and commission emergency exit monitoring devices across a 100-room hotel to <4 hours without structural door modification
Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit alarm functionality being impaired during device mounting, pairing, or initial configuration

Increase the ability to verify that every installed exit monitoring device is transmitting correctly and that the audible alarm operates without obstruction — within a single end-to-end commissioning test
Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit remaining in an unauthorized open state for >5 minutes without triggering a staff alert
Minimize the likelihood of legitimate authorized staff uses of emergency exits (housekeeping, maintenance) generating false alerts that erode security staff confidence in the monitoring system
Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit monitoring device adding any additional step or force requirement that delays occupant evacuation during a fire emergency

Increase the ability of a single security officer to monitor real-time exit door status across all floors from one centralized interface without physical patrol — covering ≥50 exit points simultaneously
Minimize the time from emergency exit activation event to security staff notification to <30 seconds regardless of time of day
Minimize the likelihood of wireless connectivity loss causing undetected monitoring gaps exceeding 15 minutes during hotel occupied hours
Minimize the likelihood of the monitoring system misclassifying an emergency evacuation event as an unauthorized door use, triggering an incorrect security response that delays fire evacuation
Minimize the time required to reset a triggered exit monitoring device and restore it to active state after investigation, to <2 minutes
Minimize the likelihood of a high-priority unauthorized exit event being missed due to alert saturation from high-frequency low-priority activations
Increase the ability to produce a complete, timestamped digital audit log of all emergency exit activations and response actions, formatted for fire marshal inspection without manual compilation
Minimize the time required to identify and proactively replace batteries approaching depletion threshold across a deployment of ≥30 exit monitoring devices before monitoring capability is lost
Minimize the effort required to integrate emergency exit monitoring event data with an existing hotel BMS or security management platform running standard building protocols
Minimize the variability in emergency exit compliance status across all floor levels at any given time of day
Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit door management violations being cited during annual fire authority inspection or franchise brand standards audit

rows.Retrieval: "Becomes the person who runs a systematic, audit-proof safety operation that never fails inspection"
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do so they CAN demonstrate systematic control?"
rows.Market Need: Increase the ability to demonstrate evidence-based emergency exit management to insurance underwriters and brand auditors with a single report — without manual data assembly [hypothesis]

rows.Retrieval: "Franchise auditors and insurers must see: this property manages safety proactively, not reactively"
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do so auditors SEE a professional, documented process?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the likelihood of a preventable emergency exit failure becoming a documented finding in a franchise brand standards audit [hypothesis]

rows.Retrieval: "Wants to feel in control; fears being responsible when an undetected exit breach contributes to a security incident"
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do to produce the feeling of continuous, reliable coverage even when alone?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the sense of uncertainty about whether unmanned emergency exits remain secured during overnight minimum-staffing periods [hypothesis]

rows.Error Statement (short): E6 — Unauthorized exit open >5 min undetected
E15 — Digital audit log, fire marshal ready

rows.Check: All statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…" with ONE measurable KPI
product_related=true only where need disappears with a different technology (e.g., guard patrol has no battery/RF need)
No uniform importance/satisfaction pattern; safety/regulatory at 8-10; operational at 7-8
Impact category matches KPI (audit log → safety; alert latency → time; battery → reliability)
≥2 needs per job step; 5/19 product-specific (26%); 6 underserved identified
Zero references to EasyWave, 868 MHz, GfS, Türwächter, or piezoelectric in need statements
Anchor at L5 — standalone system; parent L6H-SEC
Product job: "Help Building Security & Life Safety to…" includes parent VN unit and context qualifier

Wireless RF coverage mapping and gateway placement
Device installation, gateway pairing, zone configuration
Acceptance testing of alarm and radio transmission
Centralized dashboard and alert delivery — primary IoT value
Alert response, device reset, rule adjustment

Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring (Türwächter)
